fees.applied: charged only on days the strategy trades

metricsGlossary.sortino: return per unit of downside risk
metricsGlossary.pctInMarket: share of days holding a position

caveats: Each engine is one fixed rule, walk-forward style — no parameters fit to this data.
Fees modeled; equities commission-free, crypto at Kraken taker.
Research and education only. Not advice, not a promise. Losses shown.
honestSummary: Three engines across six markets, net of fees, losses shown. In this bull window none beat buy & hold on raw return — they're risk tools. Orthrus cut drawdown everywhere; Hydra helped on some markets and hurt on others; Sisyphus barely participates but loses little (except on trending assets like gold).

engines.name: Orthrus
engines.rule: Hold while price is above its 200-day average; cash below. Acts next day's close.
engines.verdict: The steady hand. Cut the worst drawdown versus buy & hold on every one of the six markets, trading rarely. It lags in rallies — the cost of sitting out dips — and bleeds on Bitcoin's chop. A risk-reducer, not an alpha machine.

engines.name: Hydra
engines.rule: Hold while the trailing 6-month return is positive; cash otherwise. Acts next day's close.
engines.verdict: Mixed, and we show it. Solid on indices and gold, but on NVDA it dug a DEEPER hole than buy & hold (-29% vs -20%) for less return, and it lagged on gold too. Momentum whipsaws volatile names. Regime-dependent, not a free lunch.

engines.name: Sisyphus
engines.rule: Buy when price falls below its lower Bollinger band (oversold); flat otherwise. Acts next day's close.
engines.verdict: The sniper. In the market only ~3-6% of the time, buying deep oversold dips. Eye-popping risk-adjusted numbers (SPY Calmar 16.8) come precisely BECAUSE it barely participates — efficient per day invested, but it captures little and gets run over on assets that just trend up (it lost on gold). Precision over participation.
